lesson.title: CAPS.PhysicalSciences.Gr11.PHY.2: Newton's first, second (Fnet = ma), and third laws. Free-...
lesson.explanation: Vectors have both magnitude and direction. To add vectors: use the head-to-tail method or resolve into components. Resolution: Fx = F cos θ, Fy = F sin θ (where θ is measured from the horizontal). The resultant = √(Rx² + Ry²) at angle tan⁻¹(Ry/Rx). On inclined planes: weight resolves into a component parallel to the slope (mg sin θ) and perpendicular (mg cos θ).

practice_problems.question: A 10 kg box is pushed with a horizontal force of 60 N on a frictionless surface. Calculate the acceleration.
hints: Use Newton's second law: F_net = ma.
On a frictionless surface, the applied force is the net force.
practice_problems.explanation: F_net = ma. 60 = 10a. a = 6 m/s^2 in the direction of the applied force.

practice_problems.question: A 5 kg block is pulled along a rough surface with a force of 40 N. The coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.3. Calculate the acceleration. (g = 10 m/s^2)
hints: First find friction: f = mu x N = mu x mg.

practice_problems.question: State Newton's third law and explain why a person does not fly backward when pushing against a wall.
practice_problems.answer: Newton's 3rd law: for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction (forces act on DIFFERENT objects). When you push the wall, the wall pushes you back with equal force. You do not fly backward because friction between your feet and the floor provides a balancing force, keeping you in equilibrium.
hints: The action-reaction pair acts on different objects.
What other forces act on the person?
practice_problems.explanation: The reaction force from the wall acts on you, but friction from the floor prevents you from accelerating. If you stood on ice (no friction), you would slide backward.
distractors: The wall's reaction force is smaller
Newton's 3rd law does not apply to stationary objects
The person has too much mass to move

practice_problems.question: Two blocks (3 kg and 5 kg) are connected by a light string on a frictionless horizontal surface. A 40 N force is applied to the 5 kg block. Find the acceleration and the tension in the string.
hints: Treat the system as a whole to find acceleration: F = (m1+m2)a.
Then isolate the 3 kg block: T = m1 x a.
practice_problems.explanation: System: 40 = 8a, a = 5 m/s^2. For the 3 kg block: only tension acts, so T = 3 x 5 = 15 N.

practice_problems.question: A 2 kg block rests on a 30-degree frictionless incline. It is connected by a string over a pulley to a 3 kg hanging mass. Find the acceleration and the direction of motion. (g = 10 m/s^2)
practice_problems.answer: Weight component of 2 kg along slope = 2(10)sin(30) = 10 N (down slope). Weight of 3 kg = 30 N (downward). Net force = 30 - 10 = 20 N. a = 20/(2+3) = 4 m/s^2. The 3 kg mass moves down, pulling the 2 kg block up the incline.
hints: For the block on the incline, only the component along the slope matters.
The system accelerates in the direction of the larger net force.
practice_problems.explanation: The 3 kg weight (30 N) exceeds the 2 kg block's weight component along the slope (10 N), so the 3 kg mass falls, accelerating the system at 4 m/s^2.

hints: What concept from Vectors in Two Dimensions applies here?
Apply the method for Vectors in Two Dimensions step by step.
Start by writing down what you know about Vectors in Two Dimensions.

misconceptions.misconception: A common error in Vectors in Two Dimensions.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: This mistake is common because students often skip a key step.

assessment.title: Assessment: Newton's first, second (Fnet = ma), and third laws. Free-body diagrams. Applications: lifts, connected objects, inclined planes with friction. Normal force and weight
